planting.note: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or nick the seed coat, then direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination. Wild Lupine develops a long, deep taproot that makes transplanting nearly impossible — choose the permanent site carefully and sow in place. Requires acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), sandy, infertile, well-drained soil; rich or clay soils will cause decline.

directions: Scarify seeds by rubbing lightly with sandpaper or nicking the hard seed coat with a knife, then direct sow in fall into permanently selected, dry, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), sandy or gravelly, infertile soil in full sun — Wild Lupine will fail in rich, clay, or wet conditions. This native perennial produces handsome palmate foliage and blue-violet flower spikes in May–June and is the sole larval host plant of the federally endangered Karner Blue butterfly, making it a conservation priority for eastern dry oak savanna and pine barren restorations. Do not attempt to transplant established plants; the deep taproot is extremely sensitive to disturbance, and plants will almost always die if moved. Once established, Wild Lupine is essentially self-sufficient, fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, and will self-seed modestly to form a small colony in suitable conditions.

planting.note: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or nick with a knife before sowing — this is essential as the hard seed coat dramatically reduces germination without scarification. Direct sow in fall (September–November in zones 7–10) into well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil; Texas Bluebonnets must overwinter as rosettes to bloom the following spring and will not flower if spring-sown. Do not sow in spring.

directions: Scarify seeds by rubbing with coarse sandpaper or nicking the hard coat with a knife, then direct sow in fall (September through November) into thin, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun — Texas Bluebonnets must overwinter as basal rosettes to set flower buds and will not bloom if sown in spring. The Texas state flower produces stunning sky-blue and white upright flower spikes in March–April, spreading along roadsides and meadows in one of the most iconic wildflower displays in North America. Like all lupines, plants fix nitrogen through root nodules and resent transplanting due to a deep taproot; sow seeds in their permanent location and thin to 6–12 inches after seedlings are established. Plants self-seed reliably where conditions suit them, building in density each year.

planting.note: Scarify seeds with sandpaper before sowing to break the hard seed coat and significantly improve germination rates. Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or sow in early spring after last frost. This species develops a deep taproot and dislikes transplanting — sow seeds in their permanent location in well-drained, slightly acidic soil.

directions: Scarify seeds by rubbing with sandpaper before sowing to break the hard seed coat and improve germination; direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or in early spring into permanently selected, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile-to-average soil in full sun. Large-Leaved Lupine is the wild Pacific Northwest perennial that served as the primary parent of the beloved 'Russell Hybrid' lupines, producing tall spikes of blue-violet, white, or bicolor pea flowers in June atop large, palmately compound leaves. This species thrives in the cool, moist summers of the Pacific Northwest and northern gardens but declines in heat and humidity; it is not a good choice for zones 7 and above. Deadhead spent spikes promptly to encourage a second flush of bloom and to prevent aggressive self-seeding, as escapes in the Pacific Northwest have become problematic in some riparian habitats.

planting.note: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or nick the seed coat before sowing, then direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination. Carolina False Lupine develops a deep taproot and resents transplanting — sow seeds in a permanent, well-drained site. Can also be sown in early spring after last frost.

directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper to break dormancy, then direct sow in fall or early spring into well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Carolina False Lupine develops a deep taproot early and should be sown in its permanent location rather than transplanted. This southeastern native perennial produces bright yellow, upright pea-flower spikes in April–May that closely resemble lupine blooms, making it an outstanding lupine substitute for the hot, humid climates of the Piedmont and Appalachian foothills where true lupines struggle. Unlike lupines, Thermopsis villosa is genuinely heat and humidity tolerant, thrives in the Southeast, and is long-lived once established, slowly spreading by rhizomes to form an impressive clump. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and requires little to no fertilization.

planting.note: Scarify seeds by rubbing with sandpaper or nicking the seed coat before sowing — scarification is essential for reliable germination. Direct sow in fall in a permanent site with well-drained, sandy or loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Cream Wild Indigo is extremely slow from seed (2–3 years to bloom) but is essentially immortal once established; choose the planting site with care as plants resent disturbance and should never be moved.

directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or nick the hard coat with a knife before fall sowing — this is critical, as unscarified Baptisia seeds have very low germination rates; direct sow into the permanent site in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun, as Cream Wild Indigo develops a deep taproot and cannot be transplanted successfully once established. This low-growing, sprawling prairie perennial produces creamy-white, downward-nodding pea-flower spikes in early spring (April–May), followed by large, inflated charcoal-black seed pods that rattle when ripe and are prized for dried arrangements. Cream Wild Indigo is one of the slowest native wildflowers to establish from seed — expect 2–3 years before first bloom — but plants are essentially immortal, extremely drought tolerant once established, fix atmospheric nitrogen, and require no care whatsoever once settled; a mature specimen is one of the finest and most trouble-free prairie plants available.
